result:
# The Weight of Small Decisions

I stood in the coffee shop for seven minutes trying to decide between medium and large. The barista waited with practiced patience, her marker hovering over the cup. Behind me, I could feel the weight of other people's mornings pressing against my back.

It's always been like this. Not just with coffee, but with everything. Which route to take home. Whether to text back immediately or wait. What to order at restaurants. If I should say what I'm actually thinking or the safer, smoother thing. Each choice branches into infinite futures I'll never see, alternate versions of my life splitting off like fractals, impossible to track.

My therapist says this is anxiety. My mother says I overthink. My ex-girlfriend said I was "exhausting to be around," which at least had the virtue of specificity.

I ordered the medium. I always order the medium.

Walking back to my apartment, I passed the same homeless man who's been on the corner of Fifth and Main for three years. I gave him the same apologetic smile I always give—acknowledging his existence while doing absolutely nothing about it. This is maybe the worst decision I make, every single day, and also the easiest. 

The thing about small decisions is that they feel both crushingly important and utterly meaningless at the same time. The coffee size doesn't matter. Except it determines how much caffeine I intake, which affects my anxiety levels, which influences how I interact with my coworker Dave, which could theoretically alter the trajectory of the project we're working on, which might impact whether I get promoted, which could change everything.

Or I just drink some coffee and nothing happens.

I've been reading about parallel universes. The many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics suggests that every decision splits reality into multiple branches, each one equally real. There's a universe where I ordered the large. One where I didn't go to the coffee shop at all. One where I said something to the barista beyond my order, where we struck up a conversation, where she became important to me somehow.

In infinite universes, I've made every possible choice.

This should be comforting. It isn't.

Because I'm stuck here, in this one, with this specific accumulation of decisions that have made me whoever I am. Thirty-four years old, still single, working a job that's fine, living in an apartment that's fine, drinking medium coffees and taking the same route home and never, ever doing anything unexpected.

My phone buzzes. It's my brother, asking if I want to come to his daughter's birthday party this weekend. It's a three-hour drive. I have things I should do this weekend—laundry, groceries, finally hanging those pictures that have been leaning against the wall for eight months. 

But also: my niece will only turn seven once. And my brother has been better to me than I've been to him. And I'm so fucking tired of my own company.

I type out "Yes," then delete it. Type "Can't this weekend," delete that too.

The barista had a tattoo on her wrist. I noticed it when she handed me the cup. Some kind of bird, wings spread. I wondered about the story behind it, if she chose it quickly or agonized over the design, the placement, the commitment of it. Whether she regrets it now or if it still means whatever it meant when she first got it.

I wish I could be the kind of person who gets tattoos. Who makes bold choices and lives with them, skin and all.

Instead I'm the kind of person who's been thinking about adopting a dog for two years but can't commit to the responsibility. Who keeps rewatching the same shows because new ones might disappoint. Who has a document on his computer titled "Novel Ideas" that hasn't been opened since 2019.

The coffee is already getting cold.

Here's what I know: every small decision is practice for the big ones. And I'm practicing cowardice. I'm rehearsing mediocrity. I'm getting incredibly skilled at choosing nothing, which is still a choice, just the worst kind.

There's a universe where I'm different. Where I said yes to the job in Seattle. Where I asked Rachel to stay instead of pretending I was fine with her leaving. Where I told my father I loved him before he died instead of assuming there would be more time.

But that's not this universe.

This is the one where I stand outside my apartment building, phone in hand, cursor blinking in an empty text message.

My thumbs move. "I'll be there. Wouldn't miss it."

I hit send before I can unsend it. Before I can qualify it or add conditions or give myself an exit route. The message goes, disappears into the digital void, becomes real in my brother's phone somewhere across the city.

My heart is pounding like I've done something dangerous.

Maybe I have.

I look at my coffee cup. The barista had written my name wrong—"Jon" instead of "John." An inconsequential error. A universe of difference. In some reality, I went back and corrected her. In this one, I keep walking, carrying someone else's name, feeling something shift in my chest that might be discomfort or might be possibility.

The homeless man is still on his corner. Tomorrow, I think. Tomorrow maybe I'll stop. Maybe bring him coffee. Maybe actually talk to him.

Or maybe I won't.

But I could.

And that, right now, feels like enough.
